content.problemStatement:
Chef is taking a tough examination. The question paper consists of NNN objective problems and each problem has 444 options A,B,C,A, B, C,A,B,C, and DDD, out of which, **exactly one** option is correct.

Since Chef did not study for the exam, he does not know the answer to any of the problems. Chef was looking nearby for help when his friend somehow communicated the following information:


Exactly NAN_ANA​ problems have option AAA as the answer.
Exactly NBN_BNB​ problems have option BBB as the answer.
Exactly NCN_CNC​ problems have option CCC as the answer.
Exactly NDN_DND​ problems have option DDD as the answer.
Note that:


NA+NB+NC+ND=NN_A + N_B + N_C + N_D = NNA​+NB​+NC​+ND​=N.
Each problem is worth exactly 111 mark and there is **no negative marking**.
Even though Chef knows the number of correct options of each type, he does not know the correct answer to **any** problem.
Based on the given information, find the **maximum** marks Chef can **guarantee** if he marks the answers optimally.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
First line of each test case contains an integer NNN denoting the number of problems.
Second line of each test case contains 444 integers NA,NB,NC,N_A, N_B, N_C,NA​,NB​,NC​, and NDN_DND​ - as mentioned in the problem statement.


Output Format
For each test case, output the **maximum** marks Chef can **guarantee** if he marks the answers optimally.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: the first line contains an integer N, the total number of problems. The second line contains four space‑separated integers NA NB NC ND — the exact numbers of problems whose correct answer is A, B, C and D respectively. It is guaranteed that NA+NB+NC+ND = N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the maximum marks Chef can guarantee, i.e. the maximum possible number of correct answers he can be sure of.

sampleTestCases.explanation: In the first test case all correct answers are C, so answering C for every question guarantees 5 correct answers. In the second test case the largest count is NA=7, therefore answering A for every question guarantees 7 correct answers.
All answers are D, so answering D for all three questions guarantees 3 correct answers.
The maximum among the counts is 2 (either A or B). Answering all A (or all B) guarantees 2 correct answers; any mixed strategy cannot guarantee more.

hidden.label: edge case all zero except one

solution.approach: Chef knows the exact count of each correct option but not their positions. If he answers all questions with the same option X, the worst case is that all non‑X answers occupy the positions he chose, leaving only the true X answers as correct. Hence he is guaranteed exactly NX correct answers. Any mixed strategy can only reduce this guarantee because the adversary can place his guesses on mismatching positions. Therefore the optimal guaranteed score is simply the maximum among NA, NB, NC and ND. The algorithm for each test case is: read N (ignored), read the four counts, output max(NA, NB, NC, ND).
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables.
